artifacts.content:
Dear Tom,

I wanted to write things down while they’re still fresh, because when we talked on the phone last night I felt a little jumbled.

The trouble at the Battle Creek PACE center on 1/15 really frightened me. When my morning medications didn’t come until about 10:45, I just kept thinking about that night back in late October 2025 at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital, when Dr. Patel, the hospitalist in the ER, told me that another bad low like that could cause permanent damage or even be fatal, especially with my two little strokes in 2019. Sitting there in the main activity room, feeling lightheaded and “off,” I remembered how it felt to stay overnight in that noisy ER and how scared you looked when you came in from Kalamazoo.

It also stirred up old feelings from March 2017 after your dad died, when Dr. Nguyen at Grace Internal Medicine first put me on sertraline and I barely got out of bed for almost three weeks until you came to Brookstone Lane to make sure I was eating. When the aide snapped, “We’re busy right now; you’ll get them when we get to you,” in front of everyone, it felt like I was being scolded instead of cared for, and that same dark, ashamed feeling crept in.

Angela Martinez has been very kind about it, and we talked again on 1/22 about the plan going forward, so I do feel somewhat better. I’ll tell you more when you’re here Saturday for groceries. Please don’t worry too much, but I thought you should know why it shook me the way it did.

Love,
Mom

metadata.subject: Catching up about the PACE scare

artifacts.content:
Journal – Friday, 2026-01-16, after breakfast

Yesterday at the Senior Care Partners PACE day center in Battle Creek was harder than I first admitted to Angela.

I arrived around 8:30, sat in the main activity room like usual, and by 9:15 I started watching the clock. My morning pills – blood pressure and diabetes – are supposed to be closer to 8:30–9:00, like Dr. Patel at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital said after that mini-stroke in March 2023. When nothing came, I reminded the nursing assistant around 9:30. Her exact words: “We’re busy right now; you’ll get them when we get to you.” Said in front of everyone, sharp, like I was a naughty child.

I felt that same shame I did in June 2023 when I had to ask Angela to go with me to Kellogg Community Credit Union to set up automatic withdrawal for my Consumers Energy bill after I let it get $182 behind and nearly had the power shut off. Losing track of money, losing track of time – it all blurs together and makes me feel like I can’t manage my own life anymore.

By about 10:30 I was lightheaded and a bit nauseated. They finally brought the meds around 10:45 with a snack. It also brought back the panic from late October 2025 at Bronson, when my blood sugar crashed and Dr. Patel warned me how dangerous another episode could be with my stroke history.

I value PACE, but I need them to understand that these delays and that tone touch old wounds – the shutoff notice, Richard’s death, the ER visits. I will bring this up again with Angela when my head is clearer.

Hi Ellen,

I know you’re busy in Grand Rapids, but I wanted to give you a fuller update than our quick call.

The medication delay at the Battle Creek PACE center on 01/15 upset me more than I let on at first. Part of it is my memory. You and I have both noticed it getting worse since around spring 2024, and when Dr. Karen Lewis at the Bronson Neuroscience Center in Kalamazoo told us in August 2025 that the testing suggested early Alzheimer’s disease, I think a part of me has been quietly terrified ever since.

So when I was sitting there in the main room on 01/15, unable to keep track of the time, and the aide brushed me off in front of everyone, it felt like they weren’t taking my cognitive problems seriously. I depend on them to keep my routine because I can’t always trust my own sense of the clock anymore.

All of that piled on top of the stress about Thomas’s Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Kalamazoo from July 2025 – that $650 a month he has to pay to keep the house weighs on me. When my blood sugar and blood pressure were off and I started to feel shaky, I kept thinking, “If something happens to me, they’ll have one more mess to deal with.”

Angela Martinez met with me privately on 1/16 and again on 1/22. She explained the changes they’re making, including checking my blood sugar before 9:00 on center days and aiming for my morning meds closer to 8:30. That helps. I also told her about that awful hypoglycemia spell at St. Philip’s last Easter when Father Michael had to stop Mass and call 911.

I’m tired but calmer now. Call when you can.

Love,
Mom

metadata.subject: A bit more about what’s been going on

artifacts.content:
Event: PACE Center Visit – Medication & Safety Focus
Date: Wednesday, 2026-01-28
Time: 8:00 AM–3:30 PM
Location: Senior Care Partners PACE day center, Battle Creek, MI

Notes for myself:

– Transportation van should pick me up at Brookstone Lane around 8:00.
– Bring walker, reading glasses, knitting, and this notebook.
– VERY IMPORTANT: Morning medications (blood pressure pills, metformin, insulin, lisinopril) should be given as close to 8:30 AM as possible, like Dr. Patel at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital recommended after my mini-stroke (TIA) in March 2023. New care plan says target 8:30–9:00.
– Ask nurse to check my blood sugar BEFORE 9:00 AM, per the Service Delivery Request Angela updated on 1/20, because of that hypoglycemia episode at St. Philip’s Catholic Church last Easter 2025 when Father Michael had to stop Mass and call 911.
– If meds seem to be running late again:
   • Politely ask the nurse directly.
   • If still delayed after 10:00, remind Angela of our agreement from 1/22 that she or the nurse will call Tom in Kalamazoo so he doesn’t panic like October 2024 when he called the Battle Creek police for a welfare check and they found me on the bathroom floor in my nightgown.
– Pay attention to how I feel between 9:00 and 11:00 (lightheaded, shaky, “off”). If anything feels wrong, speak up right away.
– Try to sit with the usual ladies for lunch and enjoy the conversation about grandchildren and recipes. This is supposed to help me stay independent, not make me afraid.

metadata.title: PACE Day – Reminder and Safety Plan
metadata.location: Senior Care Partners PACE – Battle Creek

artifacts.content:
Dear James,

Thank you again for helping me sort out the tablet last weekend. I’m proud of you, working in IT in Lansing and still making time for your old grandma.

I’ve been feeling a bit overwhelmed, and I wanted to share with you since you’re good at untangling problems. Between your dad’s Chapter 13 bankruptcy he filed in Kalamazoo in July 2025 – that $650 a month payment just to keep the house – and my own health scares, my nerves feel thin.

Do you remember me telling you about June 2024, when I passed out here at 1842 Brookstone Lane and the LifeCare Ambulance paramedics had to take me to the ER at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital because my blood sugar and blood pressure were dangerously high? Ever since then, any little wobble makes me think of sirens.

So when the PACE center in Battle Creek was nearly two hours late with my meds on 01/15, and the aide snapped, “We’re busy right now; you’ll get them when we get to you,” it all came rushing back – that June episode, and also late October 2025 when I had to stay overnight in the Bronson ER for a severe low and Dr. Patel warned me that another bad spell could be the end of me with my stroke history.

The good news is that Angela, my care coordinator, has taken it seriously. She updated my care plan so they check my blood sugar before 9:00 and aim for my pills by 8:30. The last three visits my insulin and lisinopril have been on time, and I haven’t had another spell like June 2024.

Don’t worry too much; I’m still knitting and watching my mysteries. I just wanted you to know what’s been on my mind.

Love,
Grandma

metadata.subject: Catching you up, since you always ask

artifacts.content:
Personal note – for doctor visits and to remind myself

Things that still bother me about the 01/15/26 incident at the Battle Creek PACE day center:

1. Fear from past episodes:
   – Late October 2025: overnight in the ER at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital for dangerously low blood sugar. Hospitalist Dr. Patel told me that at my age, with two minor strokes in 2019, another bad low could cause permanent damage or even be fatal.
   – June 2024: passed out at home at 1842 Brookstone Lane. LifeCare Ambulance paramedics took me to Bronson ER for very high blood sugar and blood pressure.
   – Last Easter 2025 at St. Philip’s Catholic Church: hypoglycemia during communion, Father Michael stopped Mass and called 911.

2. Memory and thinking:
   – Memory has been worsening since spring 2024.
   – August 2025: Dr. Karen Lewis at Bronson Neuroscience Center in Kalamazoo said my testing suggested early Alzheimer’s disease.
   – I depend on others to keep my routine and tell me when meds are late.

3. Feelings of shame and being a burden:
   – June 2023: Angela helped me at Kellogg Community Credit Union after I let my Consumers Energy bill get $182 behind and almost had the power shut off.
   – March 2017: after Richard died, Dr. Nguyen at Grace Internal Medicine started sertraline; I stayed in bed almost three weeks until Tom came from Kalamazoo.
   – Early 2017: still haunted by how the rehab nurse at Borgess in Kalamazoo spoke sharply to Richard when he was dying of lung cancer.

These memories pile up when someone at PACE speaks abruptly or my medications are late. Important to mention all this to Dr. Nguyen and Angela when we review my care plan.

metadata.title: Health history and feelings – to remember

artifacts.content:
Hi Melissa,

I loved the photos from Chicago – that little café by the river looks like something out of one of my mystery shows.

Things here are quieter, though my mind has been busy. Angela Martinez, my care coordinator at the PACE program, met with me again on 1/22 about the medication mix-up. I told her that as long as my morning blood pressure pills and metformin are given closer to 8:30, the way Dr. Patel at Bronson Battle Creek Hospital ordered after my mini-stroke in March 2023, I feel comfortable staying with the program and don’t plan to ask your dad to move me into that assisted living place we toured on Riverside Drive last fall.

I also told Angela that what really hurt was the way the aide spoke to me on 1/15. It reminded me too much of how the nurse on the rehab floor at Borgess in Kalamazoo talked to your grandpa when he was dying of lung cancer in early 2017. I still lie awake some nights here at Brookstone Lane thinking about that, and when someone raises their voice or sounds impatient, it all comes back.

Angela says they’ve coached the staff and changed some of their procedures – even added a note in my care plan about calling your Uncle Tom if my morning meds are running very late, so he doesn’t panic like he did in October 2024 when the Battle Creek police did that welfare check and found me on the bathroom floor.

Most days are ordinary: crossword, African violets, knitting a baby blanket for the neighbor’s grandchild. But under it all, I just want to be treated gently and for everyone to remember that these old memories are still sitting there.

Love you lots,
Grandma
